worth_it_explanation: Display & Video 360 (DV360) buys across open exchanges where low-quality and made-for-advertising (MFA) inventory competes alongside legitimate placements. Without active controls, spend reaches domains built to farm ad revenue rather than serve real audiences, and ads appear in positions a user never actually sees. These checks are fast to run inside the platform and the fixes are configuration changes, not rebuilds. Each one directly affects how much of the budget reaches a real person.
intro: Inventory quality in DV360 covers the controls that determine which sites, apps, and placements receive budget. The platform buys programmatically across a wide range of supply sources, and the default state of a new line item does not exclude MFA domains, low-viewability placements, or stale exclusion lists. These checks confirm that the advertiser has applied the available filters and that those filters are maintained over time.

audit_checks.title: Made-for-advertising sites and low-quality domains are not excluded at the advertiser or line item level
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Made-for-advertising (MFA) sites generate page views for ad revenue rather than audience engagement, so budget directed there produces impressions with no real user attention behind them. At scale, a meaningful share of programmatic spend goes to these domains when no exclusion is in place, and the performance data they return inflates click and engagement metrics without delivering real outcomes.

educate.base:
Made-for-advertising sites are properties built primarily to serve ads rather than to publish content a real audience seeks out. They generate programmatic inventory at volume, often with low-quality or scraped content, inflated page view counts, or aggressive ad density. Because they participate in open exchanges, they compete for budget on every line item that does not explicitly exclude them.

DV360 provides controls at both the advertiser level and the line item level to exclude these domains. At the advertiser level, a channel targeting list of excluded URLs or app IDs applies as a default to new line items, which removes the need to configure exclusions on every campaign individually. At the line item level, an inventory source setting can further restrict where ads run.

Google publishes its own brand safety controls through Digital Content Labels and sensitive content categories, which filter by content type rather than by URL. These controls catch new MFA domains as they are detected. A URL exclusion list supplements them by explicitly removing domains the advertiser has identified through its own analysis or through a third-party verification vendor like DoubleVerify or Integral Ad Science (IAS).

A few signs point to this check applying:
  • No advertiser-level channel exclusion list has been created or assigned to line items
  • Post-campaign domain reports show a long tail of unfamiliar or low-authority domains receiving spend
  • A third-party verification vendor flags MFA or invalid traffic from domains that are still active in the account
You may want to review the placement report for active line items and look for domains whose names or content suggest they were built to capture ad revenue rather than build an audience.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, MFA exposure carries an additional risk: product feed retargeting can surface shopping ads on these properties, associating branded product ads with low-quality content. You may want to apply the advertiser-level exclusion list before enabling dynamic product ads.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, MFA inventory inflates impression and click volume while producing no real leads, which distorts cost-per-lead benchmarks. You may want to confirm that any performance targets used for budget decisions exclude data from known MFA domains before drawing conclusions about campaign efficiency.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether an advertiser-level exclusion list exists, whether it is assigned to active line items, and whether recent placement reports show low-quality domains receiving spend.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: Open the advertiser and navigate to 'Brand safety & suitability'. Review the Digital Content Label and sensitive content category settings to see what is excluded at the account level.
Go to 'Audiences & targeting', then 'Channel lists'. Check whether an exclusion channel list of known low-quality or MFA domains has been created and whether it is assigned to active line items.
Open an active line item and select 'Inventory source'. Confirm whether any inventory exclusions are applied at the line item level in addition to the advertiser default.

Run a placement report for the past 30 days. Sort by impressions descending and scan the domain column for unfamiliar or low-authority sites. Note any domains whose names or page content suggest an MFA purpose.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether an advertiser-level exclusion channel list exists and is assigned to active line items, and whether the placement report shows MFA or low-quality domains among the top impression sources. A pass requires an exclusion list to be in place and no MFA domains surfacing in the top placement report. A fail is recorded when no exclusion list exists, or when the placement report shows domains with clear MFA characteristics regardless of whether a list is assigned.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the exclusion list status and placement report observations above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Display & Video 360 account has no advertiser-level channel exclusion list blocking made-for-advertising (MFA) sites and low-quality domains. Programmatic buying on open exchanges distributes spend across a wide range of supply sources, and without explicit exclusions, budget reaches properties built to capture ad revenue rather than build an audience. {{voice_recommend}} creating an advertiser-level exclusion channel list of identified MFA and low-quality domains, assigning it to all active line items, and reviewing the placement report on a regular cadence to add new domains as they surface.

audit_checks.title: Viewability targeting is not applied, and Active View thresholds are not set on display or video line items
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An ad that is never in view cannot drive any recall, click, or conversion. DV360 buys ad placements whose viewability rates vary widely across the open exchange, and without a viewability threshold, budget allocates to placements where a large share of impressions go unseen. The impact compounds on video line items where completion rate and cost per completed view depend on the ad being visible.

educate.base:
Active View is Google's viewability measurement standard. For display ads, a placement meets the Media Rating Council (MRC) viewability threshold when 50 percent or more of the ad's pixels are visible for at least one second. For video, the threshold is 50 percent of pixels visible for at least two seconds. DV360 can target line items to placements that meet a minimum Active View rate, which directs budget away from ad slots that are typically below the fold, in cluttered layouts, or inside content that users scroll past quickly.

Without viewability targeting, the platform bids on placements regardless of their historical viewability rate. A line item that wins impressions on high-volume but low-viewability placements can show a competitive cost per thousand (CPM) and a strong impression count while delivering little actual audience exposure.

DV360 provides Active View targeting at the line item level. The setting applies a minimum viewability threshold as a targeting criterion, which restricts the auction pool to placements that historically meet the threshold. The tradeoff is a smaller eligible inventory pool, which may raise CPMs. You may want to confirm the threshold makes sense for the campaign goal and the available budget before applying it universally.

A few signs point to this check applying:
  • Line items have no Active View targeting set and viewability rate in the reporting tab reads below 50 percent for display or below 35 percent for video
  • The placement report shows high-impression placements concentrated on pages with heavy ad density or deep in the page layout
  • A third-party measurement vendor reports viewability below the advertiser's accepted floor
You may want to review the Active View rate column in the line item report before concluding whether current placements meet the campaign's exposure goals.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser running display retargeting, viewability directly affects whether a product ad reaches a user who can act on it. A retargeting impression that is never in view wastes a valuable audience touch. You may want to apply a viewability threshold on retargeting line items before raising frequency caps.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, display and video impressions on low-viewability placements consume budget without creating the brand or offer awareness that drives form submissions. You may want to prioritize viewability targeting on awareness-stage line items where the goal is recall rather than immediate click-through.

learn_more.source_title: About Active View in Display & Video 360

investigate.intro: Check whether Active View targeting thresholds are set on active display and video line items, and review the viewability rate in the line item report to understand current exposure levels.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: Open an active display or video line item and navigate to the 'Targeting' section. Look for an Active View targeting entry. Note whether a minimum viewability threshold is set or whether the field is empty.

Open the line item's performance report and add the 'Active View: % Viewable impressions' column. Read the viewability rate for the past 30 days and compare it against the MRC standard of 50 percent for display or 35 percent for video.
In the placement report, sort by impressions descending and scan the viewability column. Note placements that are consistently below the threshold and appear in the top impression sources.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active display and video line item, whether Active View targeting is set and what the line item's viewability rate has been over the past 30 days. A line item with Active View targeting applied and a viewability rate at or above 50 percent for display (35 percent for video) passes. A line item with no targeting set and a viewability rate below those thresholds is a fail recorded with the line item name and its measured viewability rate.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Active View targeting status and the measured viewability rate for each active line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that active display and video line items on {{site_name}}'s account do not have Active View viewability thresholds applied. Without a minimum viewability target, the account bids on placements regardless of whether a real user can see the ad, and the reported impression count includes a portion of exposures that were never in view. {{voice_recommend}} setting an Active View targeting threshold on each active display and video line item, reviewing the placement report to identify and exclude the lowest-viewability placements, and monitoring the Active View rate column after the change to confirm the threshold is having the intended effect.

audit_checks.title: URL and app exclusion lists are empty, unassigned, or have not been updated in more than 90 days
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Exclusion lists that are never updated accumulate gaps as new MFA sites, low-viewability apps, and brand-unsafe properties enter the programmatic supply. A list that was current at launch grows stale, and the account effectively runs without protection against the new supply that entered the exchange after the last update.

educate.base:
DV360 uses channel lists to manage groups of URLs, apps, and YouTube channels that an advertiser wants to include or exclude from targeting. An exclusion channel list prevents line items from bidding on inventory from the domains and apps on that list. The list is effective only if it is assigned to the relevant line items and updated as new problematic inventory is identified.

Two failure modes appear in accounts where these lists exist but are not maintained. The first is an unassigned list: the advertiser created an exclusion channel list but did not assign it to new line items added after the initial setup. The second is a stale list: the list was last updated months or years ago and does not reflect the new domains and apps that post-audit placement reports have flagged. Either way, the list's presence in the account creates a false sense of protection without delivering it.

A 90-day review cadence is a reasonable minimum for active programmatic campaigns. Placement reports, third-party verification reports from vendors such as DoubleVerify or Integral Ad Science (IAS), and industry MFA blocklists each provide new domain signals that the exclusion list should absorb on that cadence.

A few signs point to this check applying:
  • The channel exclusion list shows a 'last edited' date older than 90 days for an account running active spend
  • New line items added in the past quarter have no exclusion channel list assigned
  • Placement reports from the past 30 days show domains or apps flagged in a previous review but not yet added to the exclusion list
You may want to confirm that every active insertion order (IO) and line item has the current exclusion list assigned, not just the ones that were live when the list was first created.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, app inventory deserves specific attention. Retargeting line items that run on mobile apps can reach users inside low-quality gaming or utility apps where ad engagement is accidental or incidental. You may want to review the app placement report and add poorly performing app IDs to the exclusion list on the same 90-day cadence as domain exclusions.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, brand adjacency matters alongside traffic quality. A lead-generation ad appearing next to low-quality content can associate the brand with that content in a user's perception. You may want to review the 'brand suitability' report alongside the placement report when updating the exclusion list.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether exclusion channel lists exist, whether they are assigned to all active line items, and when they were last updated.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: Open the advertiser and go to 'Audiences & targeting', then 'Channel lists'. Review the list of channel lists and note their 'Last edited' dates.

For each exclusion channel list, check whether it is assigned to the active line items. Open a sample of recently created line items and confirm the list appears in their inventory targeting settings.

Run a placement report for the past 30 days. Compare the domains and app IDs appearing in the report against the contents of the exclusion list. Note any placements flagged in earlier reviews that have not yet been added.

Check whether the account has integrated a third-party brand safety vendor (e.g., DoubleVerify or IAS). If so, review the vendor's report for domains and apps flagged since the last exclusion list update.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether an exclusion channel list exists, the date of its last update, whether it is assigned to all active line items created in the past 90 days, and whether placement reports show domains or apps that should be on the list but are not. A list updated within 90 days and assigned to all active line items passes. A list with a last-edited date older than 90 days, or one that is missing from line items created after its initial setup, is a fail recorded with the list name, last-edited date, and any newly observed domains or app IDs that belong on it.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the exclusion list status, last-edited date, and any placement report observations above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s URL and app exclusion lists in Display & Video 360 are either unassigned to active line items or have not been updated recently enough to reflect current placement report findings. An exclusion list that is not maintained loses its protective effect as new low-quality and brand-unsafe inventory enters the open exchange. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the exclusion channel list's last-edited date, updating it with domains and app IDs from the past 30-day placement report, assigning the updated list to all active insertion orders and line items, and establishing a 90-day update cadence tied to placement report reviews.
